results.possibleIdentity: Watt Wetmore Webb (August 27, 1927 – October 29, 2020) was a distinguished American biophysicist and applied physicist who spent the majority of his career at Cornell University in Ithaca, New York. He joined the Cornell faculty in 1961 as an associate professor of engineering physics and later became the S.B. Eckert Professor in Engineering and director of the School of Applied and Engineering Physics (1983–1988). Webb is most famous for the co-invention of multiphoton microscopy (MPM) in 1990 and the development of fluorescence correlation spectroscopy (FCS) in 1972, both of which revolutionized biological imaging and the study of molecular dynamics. His early scientific training was in metallurgy, receiving his ScD from MIT in 1955, and he worked at Union Carbide Corporation's research laboratories where he focused on solid-state physics, chemical physics, and the development of automated submerged arc welding. His research in the 1960s and 1970s bridged several technical fields including physics, metallurgy (specifically refractory metals), and superconductivity, where he developed the first stable superconducting magnet in 1964. He was an active figure in the interdisciplinary sciences, potentially collaborating with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization during the late 1960s and early 1970s, a period when he was deeply involved in applying physical methods like lasers and optics to biological problems. Webb was an elected member of the National Academy of Sciences, the National Academy of Engineering, and the American Academy of Arts and Sciences.
